1: 2 Peter is widely recognized as a testament or farewell discourse — a literary form well-established in Second Temple Judaism (Testament of Moses, Testament of the Twelve Patriarchs, Deut 31–33, John 13–17) in which a departing leader delivers final instructions to his community. The letter's explicit acknowledgment of imminent death (2 Peter 1:14-15) confirms this genre. Testaments typically review past history, warn of coming apostasy, exhort to fidelity, and predict the future — all features present in 2 Peter. The pseudonymous attribution debate notwithstanding, the letter presents itself as Petrine final testimony, and the testament form signals that every word carries end-of-life gravity.
2: 'Knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord' — epignōsis appears five times in 2 Peter (2 Peter 1:2, 3, 8; 2 Peter 2:20; 2 Peter 3:18), far more densely than in any other NT letter. In Second Temple Judaism epignōsis (full, experiential knowledge) was distinguished from gnōsis (bare intellectual knowledge); it implies covenant participation, not mere information. The emphasis on true versus false knowledge is the epistemological battleground of the letter: the false teachers claim wisdom (2 Peter 2:12) while lacking the genuine epignōsis that produces holy character. Grace and peace multiplied 'through knowledge' signals that experiential knowledge of Christ is the medium of divine blessing.
3: 'His divine power has given us everything we need for life and godliness through our knowledge of him who called us by his own glory and goodness.' The phrase 'divine power' (theias dynameōs) and the appeal to glory and virtue/goodness (aretēs) reflect Hellenistic-Jewish appropriation of Greek philosophical vocabulary. Aretē (excellence, virtue) was the Stoic and Platonic pinnacle of human flourishing; 2 Peter applies it to God himself, claiming that God's own excellence is the means and ground of the community's godly life. The letter consistently translates Jewish theological content into categories familiar to Greco-Roman philosophy without surrendering the Jewish-Christian substance.
4: 'Partakers of the divine nature' (theias koinōnoi physeōs) — this is the most explicitly Hellenistic formulation in the NT. Greek philosophy, particularly Middle Platonism and Stoicism, spoke of the goal of human life as homoiōsis theō (likeness to God) through participation in the divine logos or nous. 2 Peter appropriates this vocabulary for Jewish-Christian ends: the divine nature shared is not absorption into deity (as in Neoplatonism) but moral participation through promise — the same promises about resurrection, the Spirit, and the new creation that ground all NT ethics. The corruption (phthoras) to be escaped is the moral corruption of epithymia (desire), not the material world.
5: The virtue-chain (sorites) of 2 Peter 1:5-7 belongs to a well-established Hellenistic literary form: the chain-argument that links virtues progressively, each building on the prior, toward a culminating virtue (here, love). Stoic diatribe used similar lists to demonstrate the systematic nature of virtue. The Hellenistic-Jewish philosophical tradition (Philo of Alexandria, Wisdom of Solomon) adapted the genre for Jewish ethics. 2 Peter's chain begins with faith and ends with love, situating distinctly Christian virtues (faith, brotherly affection, agapē) within a framework that Greco-Roman philosophical readers would find immediately recognizable as a discourse on the telos (goal) of human development.
6: Enkrateia (self-control) was the Stoic cardinal virtue governing the mastery of passions — Epictetus and Marcus Aurelius identified it as the foundation of philosophical life. In the context of 2 Peter, where the false teachers are characterized precisely by their lack of self-mastery ('following the corrupt desire of the flesh,' 2 Peter 2:10; 'eyes full of adultery,' 2 Peter 2:14), the virtue-chain's inclusion of enkrateia is polemically pointed. The false teachers who claim philosophical or spiritual sophistication lack the most basic philosophical prerequisite. Perseverance (hypomonē) adds the Jewish-covenantal virtue to the Hellenistic list: patience-under-trial as the distinctive mark of the people of God.
7: The culmination of the virtue chain in agapē (love) follows the Pauline prioritization (1 Cor 13: 'the greatest of these is love') but also the wisdom tradition's identification of love as the fulfillment of Torah (Leviticus 19:18, cited throughout the NT). Philadelphia (brotherly affection) belongs to the Greek world of kinship-based loyalty; agapē is the specifically Christian expansion of that loyalty beyond the in-group. The sequence — faith, virtue, knowledge, self-control, perseverance, godliness, brotherly affection, love — traces the movement from initial trust to the comprehensive outward orientation of love.
8: The warning about being 'ineffective and unproductive' (argous kai akarpous) in knowledge employs agricultural metaphors familiar from both wisdom literature and the teaching of Jesus (the Parable of the Sower, John 15:1-8). The Second Temple assumption was that genuine knowledge of God produces observable moral fruit; a claim to spiritual knowledge that generates no practical holiness was self-refuting. This mirrors the critique of the false teachers in 2 Peter 2, whose knowledge (2 Peter 1:20: 'escaped the corruption of the world by knowing the Lord') did not produce the fruit of transformed character and led them back into the very vices they had escaped.
9: 'Blind' and 'shortsighted' (typhlos, myōpazōn) — blindness is a stock OT image for spiritual insensibility: Isaiah's 'blind servant' (Isaiah 42:18-20), Jeremiah 5:21 ('eyes but they do not see'), and the prophetic tradition of spiritual blindness as covenant unfaithfulness. Jesus applies the image to Pharisees who claimed to see (John 9:40-41). The 'forgetting' of baptismal cleansing from sins is significant: in the Second Temple period, forgetting the covenant mercies was the root failure of apostasy (Deuteronomy 8:11-20; Ps 106). The false teachers of 2 Peter 2 represent this forgetfulness systematized into lifestyle.
10: 'Confirm your calling and election' (bebaian hymon tēn klēsin kai eklogēn poieisthai) — klēsis (calling) and eklogē (election) are the twin Pauline terms for the divine initiative in salvation (Romans 8:28-30; 2 Peter 9:11; Ephesians 1:4-5). The exhortation to 'make it sure/firm' does not undermine divine election but reflects the Second Temple Jewish conviction that election is confirmed through the life it produces — that God's choice is visible in the character of the chosen. The Dead Sea Scrolls community (1QS) similarly emphasized that election manifested in strict observance; 2 Peter's concern is with the character-confirmation of election rather than its abstract certainty.
11: 'Rich welcome into the eternal kingdom' (plousios epichorēgēthēsetai) — epichorēgeō means to provide lavishly; the same root is used in 2 Peter 1:5 ('make every effort to supplement your faith'). The lavish provision metaphor belongs to the patronage culture of the Roman world: wealthy patrons provided spectacularly for those in their household. The eternal kingdom of Jesus Christ as the eschatological household of divine patronage reverses the social calculus of 2 Peter 1:5-7: the virtue-building investment in the present age is rewarded by the patron's lavish welcome at the gate.
12: Peter's commitment to repetition and reminding ('I will always remind you... even though you know them') reflects the Jewish pedagogical tradition of hazkarah (memorial reminder): rehearsing foundational truths not because the audience is ignorant but because the act of recollection is itself formative. Deuteronomy embodies this pattern — Moses repeatedly rehearses the Sinai events for a generation that was not there. The homily and synagogue tradition carried this into Second Temple Judaism: liturgical memory of the Exodus (Passover), the giving of Torah (Shavuot), and divine rescue was constitutive of Jewish identity.
13: 'In the tent of this body' (skēnōma) — the body-as-tent image appears in Paul (2 Corinthians 5:1-4: 'if this earthly tent we live in is destroyed, we have a building from God') and here with the same implication: temporary, mortal, awaiting eschatological transformation. The tent-tabernacle imagery carries exodus freight: Israel lived in tents in the wilderness, and the Tabernacle was the tent of divine dwelling. The human body as temporary dwelling is the foil for the permanent resurrection body and the eternal kingdom of 2 Peter 1:11. Peter's tent-language signals he stands in the same theological tradition as Paul.
14: 'As our Lord Jesus Christ has made clear to me' — the reference is to John 21:18-19, where the risen Jesus predicted Peter's manner of death ('when you are old you will stretch out your hands, and another will dress you and carry you where you do not want to go'). This creates an explicit link between 2 Peter and the Johannine tradition. The foreknowledge of martyrdom by divine revelation is a prophetic convention: the prophets sometimes received anticipatory knowledge of their own death (Isaiah 8:19-22; Jeremiah 20:7-18). For Peter, it confirms the letter's testimony: his eyewitness authority (2 Peter 1:16-18) is validated by the same Lord who foretold his end.
15: 'After my departure' (meta tēn emēn exodon) — exodos (departure, lit. 'going out') was used in Jewish tradition for Moses's death (Wis 2 Peter 7:6; cf. Luke 9:31: the Transfiguration conversation about the 'exodus' Jesus was about to accomplish in Jerusalem). By using exodos for his own death, Peter may consciously invoke the Moses-departure typology: as Moses left the Torah-covenant as his permanent legacy, Peter leaves this letter. The testament genre (see 2 Peter 1:1 note) regularly positioned the dying leader as Moses-figure. The concern for post-Petrine memory is a concern for community cohesion after the apostolic generation.
16: 'Cleverly devised myths' (sesophismenois mythois) — mythos in Greek philosophical tradition (from Plato onward) meant a story that was illustrative but not factually historical, in contrast to logos (reasoned account) or historia (eyewitness report). The false teachers apparently accused the apostolic message of being myth — a common Hellenistic charge against religious claims. Peter's counter-claim is autopsy: we were eyewitnesses (epoptai). Epoptai was the technical term for initiation into the highest mysteries of the Greek mystery religions (Eleusinian mysteries: epopteia was the final level of initiation). Peter claims a superior and unrepeatable vision: the Transfiguration. The polemical register is unmistakable — apostolic testimony beats mystery-religion initiation.
17: The Transfiguration on the 'holy mountain' (2 Peter 1:18) — Peter identifies the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36) as the unique validating event for apostolic preaching about the Parousia. The 'Majestic Glory' (megaloprepous doxēs) — a divine title that appears in Deuteronomy 33:26 LXX and Jewish liturgical texts for the divine glory-cloud (Shekinah). The voice from the cloud ('This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' — the divine attestation of the Davidic kingship psalm, Psalms 2:7, combined with the Servant of YHWH, Isaiah 42:1) ratified Jesus's messianic identity in the hearing of eyewitnesses.
18: The 'sacred mountain' (hagiō orei) — in Second Temple tradition, mountains were sites of divine revelation: Sinai (Torah), Zion (Temple), Horeb (Elijah's theopany in 1 Kgs 19). Peter's 'sacred mountain' frames the Transfiguration in the tradition of Mosaic mountain-theophany. Moses received the Torah at Sinai amid divine glory; Peter, James, and John received the apostolic confirmation of Jesus's divine sonship amid a parallel glory-cloud and divine voice. The structural parallel to Sinai is deliberate and theologically loaded: as the Torah came with divine attestation on the holy mountain, the apostolic gospel came with divine attestation on the Transfiguration mountain.
19: 'The prophetic message as something completely reliable, and you will do well to pay attention to it, as to a light shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts.' The 'morning star' (phōsphoros) is Venus as it rises before dawn — in Greco-Roman tradition the Lucifer/Phosphorus star that precedes the sun. Jewish eschatology used the dawn as a metaphor for the messianic age (Numbers 24:17: 'a star will come out of Jacob'; Malachi 4:2: 'the sun of righteousness will rise'). The darkness-to-dawn metaphor for the period between the prophets and the Parousia reflects Jewish sapiential tradition: the period between revelations is a dark waiting time; the scripture is the lamp (Psalms 119:105) until the promised light arrives.
20: 'No prophecy of Scripture came about by the prophet's own interpretation' (idias epilyseōs) — epilysis (interpretation, solution, unloosing) was used in Greek literary theory for the individual author's private interpretation of dreams or oracles. The claim that Scripture is not of private (idias) origin applies equally to the prophet's composition and the reader's interpretation: neither the prophets themselves nor readers can authorize private, self-serving readings of prophetic texts. This directly challenges the false teachers' hermeneutical practice — 2 Peter 3:16 will accuse them of 'twisting' Scripture to their own ends. The criterion is the Spirit-moved community reading against the private individual's interpretation.
21: 'Prophets, though human, spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit' (hypo pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi) — pheromenoi (carried, driven, borne along) is the word used for a ship driven by wind (Acts 27:15, 17). The Spirit-as-wind-driving-prophecy image is consistent with the OT experience: the Spirit comes upon prophets as a wind/breath (ruah), and they speak beyond their own understanding. But pheromenoi does not imply unconscious dictation — prophets remained themselves while being carried. The image is of active human agency sustained and directed by the divine wind, not automatic writing. Jewish tradition (Philo, Josephus, Sibylline Oracles) similarly described prophetic inspiration as divine breath animating the human vessel.
